title: Risk-Sharing and the Term Structure of Interest Rates
abstract: I propose a general equilibrium model with heterogeneous investors to explain the key properties of the U.S. real and nominal term structure of interest rates. I find that differences in investors' elasticities of intertemporal substitution are critical in accounting for the dynamics of nominal and real yields. The nominal term structure is driven primarily by real shocks so that it can be upward sloping regardless of the correlation between nominal and real shocks.

article.introduction:
Characterizing the macroeconomic fundamentals drivingthe real and nominal yield curves in a unified framework has been a long-standing challenge for macroeconomists and financial economists (Gürkaynak and Wright (2012)). The main contribution of this paper is to propose a model in which investors' incentives to borrow from and lend to each other play a key role in understanding the salient properties of U.S. real and nominal yield curves.
I build on a general equilibrium term structure model populated by investors with heterogeneous preferences who use a risky asset and a risk-free short-term bond to share risks and smooth consumption. The central message of the paper is that accounting for differences in investors' willingness to substitute consumption over time is critical in capturing the main empirical properties of the nominal and real yield curves.
I follow an extensive literature1by assuming that attitudes toward risk differ across investors, and thus, their risk exposures also differ. In particular, Wang (1996) studies the implications of this assumption over the yield curve in a setup in which investors feature time-additive constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) utilities with different risk-aversion (RA) coefficients. My setup differs from Wang (1996) in two ways. First, I use recursive preferences. I do so because separating investors' RA from their elasticity of intertemporal substitution (EIS) is critical to balance the intertemporal substitution and precautionary savings forces driving interest rate dynamics and rationalize the observed yield curve facts. Second, I use an overlapping generation (OLG) structure following Gârleanu and Panageas (2015) and Blanchard (1985). A common feature in models with heterogeneous agents—for example, those in which agents have different RA coefficients such as Wang (1996)—is that they are nonstationary, that is, one class of agent dominates in the long run. The OLS structure induces stationarity, allowing the model's predictions to be confronted with the evidence.
The mechanism in the model is as follows. A negative aggregate shock causes a reduction in the fraction of net worth held by less risk-averse investors because they choose to be leveraged in equilibrium. Asset prices adjust, reflecting the new distribution of relative net worth in the economy, in which investors with a high RA and, importantly, a low EIS are now more relevant in clearing markets than they were before the negative shock occurred. The marginal agent's low EIS produces strong intertemporal smoothing that results in a higher real interest rate, whereas the high RA translates into a higher price of risk.2The increase in the price of risk in states in which real bond prices are low (i.e., high real rates) implies that the marginal investor demands a positive term premium to hold long-term bonds, producing an average upward-sloping term structure. With CRRA preferences, particularly those close to log preferences (to avoid an unrealistically high level of rates) as in Wang (1996), the interest rate goes down in high-marginal-utility states, resulting in a downward-sloping average yield curve.
After studying the main theoretical underpinnings of the mechanism, I analyze the nominal term structure of interest rates. To do so, I consider two alternative inflation processes: exogenous and endogenous (derived with a Taylor rule). A key property of both inflation models is that expected inflation is higher when real rates are higher.
In the exogenous inflation case, the nominal component is driven by the exogenous negative correlation between real and inflation shocks (e.g., Cox, Ingersoll, and Ross (1985), Piazzesi and Schneider (2006), Wachter (2006), Bansal and Shaliastovich (2013), among others). That is, assuming inflation occurs in bad states implies that the marginal investor requires a premium to hold nominal bonds. The real component is driven by the endogenous dynamics in investors' net worth, which drives real yields. In this model, I find that the real component explains 80% of the average nominal term premium. Put differently, the model implies that nominal risks play a secondary role in driving the nominal yield curve, a result that is in line with recent studies showing the importance of the real component in the nominal term structure (e.g., Abrahams et al. (2016), Duffee (2018)).
Motivated by the result in the exogenous inflation model, in which real risks account for the vast majority of nominal premiums, I derive a nominal term structure that is driven purely by the real component. Using a Taylor rule, I derive an endogenous inflation process that is consistent with both the policy rule and the marginal investor's nominal pricing kernel. In this case, inflation does not introduce new shocks, as in the exogenous case—that is, the nominal term premium is not driven by nominal risk. I obtain an average slope of the nominal term structure that is in line with the data, driven by the fact that the Taylor loading on the policy rule is greater than one. I show that the larger (smaller) the Taylor loading, the smaller (larger) the mean and volatility of inflation and the flatter (steeper) the nominal term structure.
Besides capturing the key moments of the real and nominal yield curves, the model is consistent with several important elements of the term structure's empirical evidence. A crucial prediction of the model is the presence of an endogenously positive and time-varying term premium, a feature that translates into a violation of the expectation hypothesis (Campbell and Shiller (1991)). Using the regression analysis by Campbell and Shiller (1991), I find that the model's predictions for the violation of the expectation hypothesis is qualitatively and quantitatively similar to that in the data. Another important implication of the model is that inflation news is relatively less important in driving the fluctuations in nominal yields. This feature is consistent with the evidence and analysis presented in Duffee (2018). Using the methodology proposed by Duffee (2018), I find that both the endogenous and the exogenous inflation versions of the model produce inflation variance ratios (the variance of inflation news divided by the variance of nominal yields' news) far below one. As noted by Duffee (2018), many leading macrofinance term structure models predict that nominal yields are driven mainly by inflation and have a hard time-generating substantial news in real rates or term premiums, thus producing variance ratios close to one. The heterogeneous agent model I propose produces highly nonlinear and persistent real rates and can capture this feature of the data relatively well, suggesting, as indicated by Duffee (2018), that it is a promising avenue for further term structure research.
To further test the model's theoretical predictions, I next extract shocks from macroeconomic data and feed these shocks into the model to study the predictions for the endogenous variables. I compare fluctuations in the model-implied series for real and nominal yields, as well as bond return volatility and excess returns, with their counterparts in the data. The model-implied series can rationalize a good amount of the observed fluctuations in the data up to the Global Financial Crisis. After 2009, the model implies persistently high yields that are counterfactual with the evidence.
This paper fits into two strands of literature: heterogeneous agents models with heterogeneous preferences and macrofinance models of the term structure. First, this paper is related to studies in macroeconomics and finance that depart from the representative agent paradigm for understanding equilibrium asset prices. A common theme in these papers is that agents exhibit heterogeneous exposure to aggregate risks (i.e., some agents operate with leverage in equilibrium) because agents are different along certain dimensions (preferences, productivity, menu of assets, beliefs, information, etc.). Within this strand, this paper is in line with studies that focus on preference heterogeneity and analyze the positive implications for asset prices and macroeconomic quantities with a frictionless credit market (e.g., Dumas (1989), Wang (1996), Chan and Kogan (2002), Bhamra and Uppal (2009), Longstaff and Wang (2012), Gârleanu and Panageas (2015), Hall (2016), Barro et al. (2017), and Schneider (2022)).3
Relative to this first strand of the literature, the contribution of this paper is to study both the real and the nominal term structures in a unified framework. In doing so, I highlight the importance of differences in investors' EIS in driving yield curve dynamics. In fact, in the results, I show that if investors differ only along the RA dimension (i.e., they have the same EIS but a different RA), the economy exhibits a downward-sloping real term structure and yields do not fluctuate much. The reason is that precautionary savings push interest rates down in high-marginal-utility states, which implies that bond prices are good hedges against negative shocks.
This paper is also related to macrofinance models of the term structure, which typically feature a representative agent. This literature is extensive, but leading examples within the long-run risk paradigm are Piazzesi and Schneider (2006) and Bansal and Shaliastovich (2013). A central feature of those papers is that the real term structure is downward sloping, and thus, they require a considerably negative correlation between inflation and real growth to obtain an upward-sloping nominal term structure. Put differently, a positive—and large—inflation premium has to more than compensate for the negative real premium to obtain an upward-sloping average nominal yield curve. As a consequence, these types of models rely heavily on persistent shocks to inflation as a determinant of long-term nominal yields—a feature that is at odds with the data (Duffee (2018)). Additionally, the volatility of long-term yields is rather low in comparison with the data, a feature that is pervasive in representative agent economies. Lastly, representative agent models display a tension between the equity premium and the term premium (Backus, Chernov, and Zin (2014)). Relative to this literature, this paper proposes a mechanism that links agents' incentives to borrow from and lend to each other with the determination of the nominal and real term structures. The mechanism generates highly persistent and nonlinear dynamics in real rates, a feature that is central to capture the salient properties of real and nominal yields (without ignoring the equity premium). By definition, the connection between borrowing and lending and the term structure cannot be made in a representative agent setup and thus departing from such a paradigm is essential.4
Within the representative agent literature, Wachter (2006) introduces exogenously time-varying habit in the framework of Campbell and Cochrane (1999) and finds upward-sloping nominal and real term structures of not only yields, but also their corresponding volatilities (the model predicts that the five-year yield is more volatile than the one-year yield). In the data, however, the longer the maturity of the bond (either nominal or real), the smaller the volatility of the yield. Also, Wachter's model predicts slopes of the nominal and real-yield curves of similar magnitude. In the data, discussed below, the slope of the nominal term structure is about twice the slope of the real term structure—the nominal term structure is steeper than the real. My model produces dynamics that are similar to those in the habits model, but are driven by endogenous fluctuations in agents' relative wealth dynamics instead of exogenous changes in habit. Additionally, relative to Wachter (2006), the model that I propose can capture the volatility of long-term yields and the fact that the nominal term structure is steeper than the real. Indeed, I show that the slope of the nominal term structure vis-à-vis the real can be rationalized by the reaction of monetary policy to an (endogenous) inflation process.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIreviews the motivating evidence. SectionIIlays out the model. SectionIIIdiscusses the model's solution and the characterization of the equilibrium. SectionIVdiscusses the term structure of interest rates in the model. SectionVshows quantitative implications. Finally, SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Preliminary Evidence: U.S. Nominal an:
This section documents the salient properties of the U.S. real and nominal yield curves described in the introduction.5In the quantitative part of the model, I seek to match this evidence. I elaborate on the evidence for nominal and real yields, for different maturities, and for three different samples. I report these data at a quarterly frequency in TableI. The source for nominal rates is Gürkaynak, Sack, and Wright (2007). For real rates, I consider TIPS from Gürkaynak, Sack, and Wright (2010) for 2003:Q1 to 2016:Q4, extended with the real rates from Chernov and Mueller (2012) for 1971:Q3 to 2002:Q4.
The Full Sample (Panel A) is from 1971:Q3 to 2016:Q4, the same data as Backus, Boyarchenko, and Chernov (2018) but extended to 2016. I then consider two subsamples. Short Sample I (Panel B) focuses on the period reported by Gürkaynak, Sack, and Wright (2010), 2003:Q3 to 2016:Q4, so it excludes the data from Chernov and Mueller (2012). Short Sample II (Panel C) excludes the Great Recession (1971:Q1 to 2007:Q4), that is, the period with unconventional monetary policy in which the short-term nominal rate was set to zero.
The main conclusion from the evidence is that the real and nominal term structures share similar properties: long-term yields have higher means and slightly smaller volatilities than short-term yields. Also, the average slope of the nominal term structure is approximately twice the slope of the real term structure, which indicates that the nominal yield curve may be capturing additional sources of risks.
article.II. Model:
In this section, I study an endowment economy populated by heterogeneous investors, and I assume that the sole source of heterogeneity among investors is their preferences. In particular, investors differ in their RA and EIS. I provide a sensitivity analysis regarding this assumption and highlight the importance of heterogeneous EISs for capturing the term structure dynamics.
The economy is populated by two classes of investors,AandB. The aggregate population remains constant and normalized to one. To obtain a stationary solution in the model, I follow Gârleanu and Panageas (2015) and consider a simple OLG framework in line with Blanchard (1985). Investors face an exogenous death riskφ>0$\varphi >0$, and a fraction φ of new investors are born. The probability φ is the same for all investors regardless of age, preference, or wealth. Of the newly born investors, a constant fractionx¯∈(0,1)$\overline{x}\in (0,1)$is of typeA, while1−x¯$1-\overline{x}$is of type B. Newly born investors receive a “start-up” endowment, perfectly tradable, to start trading in financial and goods markets.
Intuitively, since less risk-averse investors operate with leverage in equilibrium, their net worth grows faster when there is a sequence of positive returns. This implies that they can end up dominating the economy (or disappearing, if the sequence of shocks is sufficiently negative). The OLG setup prevents this outcome without changing the fundamental risk-sharing properties driven by preference heterogeneity.6
To insure against exogenous death risk, investors can write contracts with perfectly competitive insurance companies. The possibility of insuring against death risk, together with the financial instruments specified below, implies that this economy has complete markets. The contract specifies that the investor receives a flow of resources φ, proportional to the investor's net worth, per unit of time. In exchange, the investor agrees to pay the entire net worth to the insurance company at the time of death. Investors find it optimal to sign this contract provided that they have no bequest motives (Blanchard (1985)). As I show below, this device is useful to introduce stationarity in the model. I next introduce investors' preferences and balance sheets.

article.III. Solving for the Equilibrium:
Proposition 1. (Law of motion forx)The endogenous state variablexfollows an Itô process,


Proof.See theAppendix.□$\Box$

Notice that the second term in the drift functionμx,t$\mu _{x,t}$is due to the demographic structure assumed earlier. This term is key for obtaining an invariant distribution ofx. Informally, notice that for small values ofx, the diffusion tends to zero, and the drift becomes larger and positive. Thus, the process never reaches zero. Similar logic implies an upper boundary at one.7The diffusion term,σx,t$\sigma _{x,t}$, depends on the differences in investors' portfolio shares. If theαi,t$\alpha _{i,t}$'s were the same for both investors, thendW1,t$dW_{1,t}$shocks would not affectx. As a result, when the economy reaches the stochastic steady state (i.e., whenμx,t=0$\mu _{x,t}=0$), it remains there. This implies that differences in investors' exposure to aggregate risk are critical for obtaining fluctuations in the wealth distribution in this setup.
Proposition 2. (Leverage and risk-sharing)(1) A-type investors' demand for risky assets is given by


Proof.See theAppendix.□$\Box$

Discussion of Assumption 1. A large literature documents heterogeneity in EIS among individuals (Guvenen (2006) provides a summary). In general, the evidence in the literature shows that individuals who choose to be more exposed to aggregate risk (for example, by holding stocks) exhibit a larger EIS. Assumption 1 follows this line. In the model, low-RA investors choose to be more exposed to aggregate risk, and they have a larger EIS. Heterogeneity in RA is important because it implies that aggregate risk is concentrated in less risk-averse investors, and therefore,xincreases after a positive endowment shock (i.e.,σx>0$\sigma _{x}>0$). Put differently,xwould not react to macro shocks ifγA=γB$\gamma _{A}=\gamma _{B}$. Differences in RA are not the only way to obtain leveraged agents in equilibrium. For example, limited market participation immediately implies that stockholders (i.e., those who are allowed to trade the risky asset) concentrate aggregate risk (e.g., Guvenen (2009)).
The assumption is qualitatively in line with time-additive preferences featuring CRRA preferences. Under CRRA preferences, the EIS is set to the inverse of the RA coefficient. Thus, the assumption ofγA<γB$\gamma _{A}<\gamma _{B}$would immediately lead toψB<ψA$\psi _{B}<\psi _{A}$, as stated in Assumption 1. This assumption is consistent with Longstaff and Wang (2012), Wang (1996), and Hall (2016), among others. However, as I discuss later, separating the EIS from RA is critical to obtain highly nonlinear dynamics in the real rates and therefore to rationalize the properties of the yield curve observed in the data. Put differently, the EIS heterogeneity implied by the CRRA preferences is not enough because it does not produce sufficient nonlinear fluctuations in the real interest rate.8
article.IV. Term Structure of Interest Rates:
Equipped with the equilibrium definition and the model's solution, I can now characterize the term structure of interest rates in the economy. Since the economy features complete markets and there are no arbitrage opportunities, I can obtain a stochastic discount factor “as if” there were a representative agent. The properties of the discount factor, characterized in Proposition3, depend on the risk-sharing dynamics of the economy. After deriving the discount factor, I value zero-coupon bonds, where I consider both real and nominal bonds.
Proposition 3. (Stochastic discount factor)The state-price processm(xt)>0$m(x_{t}) >0$satisfies



Proof.See theAppendix.□$\Box$

PROBLEM 1. (Valuing real bonds)The price of the real bondP(x,T)$P(x,T)$(a T-real bond) solves:

Nominal Term Structure: Exogenous Inflation. I first consider the case in which inflation is exogenous, as in other papers in the macrofinance term structure literature (e.g., Piazzesi and Schneider (2006), Bansal and Shaliastovich (2013), among others). That is, I compute the nominal stochastic discount factor—which is used to discount future cash flows denominated in dollars—by introducing exogenous fluctuations in the purchasing power of a dollar (i.e., exogenous fluctuations in the price level).
The objective is to study the role of inflation risk, since in this case, the nominal term premium is driven by the assumption that inflation and real shocks are negatively correlated: Inflation occurs in high-marginal-utility states. In other words, this assumption implies that the purchasing power of nominal payments decreases precisely when the marginal investor values those resources the most. Therefore, the market has to compensate the marginal investor with a premium to hold such an asset. In the quantitative analysis later, I provide a decomposition of the nominal term premium to quantify the role of this negative correlation vis-à-vis the real component.
I assume that shocksW1andW3are correlated, that is,⟨dW1dW3⟩t=ϕ13dt$\langle dW_{1}dW_{3}\rangle _{t}=\phi _{13}dt$. In particular, I assume thatϕ13<0$\phi _{13}<0$, so shocks to π and shocks to aggregate endowment are negatively correlated (Piazzesi and Schneider (2006)). This implies that a nominal asset is expected to produce lower real payments in periods of low growth, which creates persistent inflation risk. I assume that contemporaneous shocks to the CPI process are uncorrelated withW1andW3. Similarly, I assume thatW2andW3are uncorrelated without loss of generality.

Both terms in (23), the real and the nominal, are positive. The real component is positive primarily becausePx$,(T)>0$P_{x}^{ \$,(T) }>0$∀(x,π,T)$\forall (x,\pi ,T)$, and the intuition is the same as that described earlier for the real bond. The sign of the nominal component, however, depends on the sign of the correlation between endowment shocks and inflation expectation shocks, ϕ13. This is becausePπ$,(T)<0$P_{\pi }^{\$,(T) }<0$∀(x,π,T)$\forall (x,\pi ,T)$: An increase in inflation expectations increases the spot nominal rate. Thus, the price of a nominal bond decreases when inflation expectations increase, that is, the derivative with respect to π is negative across the state space. But sinceϕ13<0$\phi _{13}<0$, positive real shocks are associated with negative shocks to inflation expectations. Economically, this means that nominal payments are expected to be eroded by inflation during periods in which investors value those resources the most. Thus, the nominal component of the nominal term premium,ϕ13Pπ$,(T)$\phi _{13}P_{\pi }^{\$,(T) }$, is positive.
With the derivedπt=π(xt)$\pi _{t}=\pi (x_{t})$, I can value nominal bonds. It is worth emphasizing that inflation is not a state variable to value nominal bonds, as in the exogenous inflation case. Instead, the sole state variable (other than time to maturity) isxt$x_{t}$, that is,Pt$,(T)=P$(x,T)$P_{t}^{\$,(T) }=P^{\$}(x,T)$. This implies that the problem of valuing nominal bonds is similar to (19).
PROBLEM 3. (Valuing nominal bonds: Endogenous inflation)The price of the nominal bondP$(x,T)$P^{\$}(x,T)$, a T-nominal bond when inflation is endogenous, solves


Before proceeding to the quantitative analysis, it is worth stressing that under the representative agent benchmark of this model, real yields are constant and exhibit zero volatility. This is a useful benchmark because it indicates that all variation in rates in the heterogeneous setup is endogenously driven by investors' risk-sharing activities.
article.V. Quantitative Analysis:
In this section, I explore the quantitative properties of the model. To that end, I solve the model and the corresponding partial differential equations for bond prices numerically. I use a global solution technique based on spectral methods (Trefethen (2000), Boyd (2001)), which I describe in theAppendix. I first discuss the calibration procedure and the model's solution. I then continue with an analysis of the real term structure and conclude by studying the nominal term structure.
Calibration. I report the calibration at a quarterly frequency in TableII. I divide parameters into the following groups: preferences, endowment and demography, and inflation.
Regarding preferences, there are mainly four parameters:γA,γB,ψA$\gamma _{A}, \gamma _{B},\psi _{A}$, andψB$\psi _{B}$. I setγB=10>γA=1.5$\gamma _{B}=10>\gamma _{A}=1.5$, which implies, on average, an aggregate γ of 5.1.11These values for RA are within the range used in the asset pricing literature. Regarding the EIS, I explore alternative plausible values with the objective of underscoring the importance of such a parameter. Intuitively, the larger the difference betweenψA$\psi _{A}$andψB$\psi _{B}$(ceteris paribus), the larger the increase in the spot real rate whenxdecreases. In the baseline calibration, I setψA=0.7$\psi _{A}=0.7$andψB=0.02$\psi _{B}=0.02$. Notice that this implies that more risk-averse agents prefer late resolution of uncertainty (γB<1/ψB$\gamma _{B}<1/\psi _{B}$), while less risk-averse agents prefer early resolution of uncertainty (γA>1/ψA$\gamma _{A}>1/\psi _{A}$). Later, I provide a sensitivity analysis and show that qualitative results are not driven by investors' preference for resolution of uncertainty. In fact, both agents can feature preference for early resolution of uncertainty, which is typically used in the long-run risk literature (see Bansal and Yaron (2004)), and the term structure properties would be qualitatively similar to those in the baseline calibration. Quantitatively, however, a lowψB$\psi _{B}$plays a key role in generating the nonlinear dynamics needed to rationalize the term structure dynamics.
I set the endowment parameters μ and σ following other papers in the heterogeneous agent literature (Chan and Kogan (2002), Gârleanu and Panageas (2015), Ehling et al. (2018), and others), to match the dynamics of U.S. real per capita consumption in the historical samples spanning the 1900 to 2016 period.12,13Regarding demographic parameters, I set a value of φ such that investors have an expected time operating in the financial market of 30 years. Lastly, I setx¯=0.11$\overline{x}=0.11$, which stabilizes the share of risk-tolerant agents' net worth around 0.15 (I report the invariant distribution later). Finally, I specify the income profile for the newly born agents asG(t)=G1e−g1t+G2e−g2t$G(t) =G_{1}e^{-g_{1}t}+G_{2}e^{-g_{2}t}$, following Gârleanu and Panageas (2015). This is a deterministic function, which is very tractable in the numerical solution and, as discussed in Gârleanu and Panageas (2015), is consistent with the evidence on individuals' earning processes. I set(G1;G2)=(30.72/4;−30.29/4)$(G_{1};G_{2})=(30.72/4; -30.29/4 )$, which implies a similar pattern to that of Gârleanu and Panageas (2015) but at a quarterly frequency. Additionally, this income profile does not affect the risk-sharing properties of the model.
To derive the endogenous inflation expectations, I calibrate the Taylor coefficientδπ$\delta _{\pi }$=1.5 as a baseline (see Taylor (1993), among many others). I also provide results for alternative values ofδπ$\delta _{\pi }$, which is the only parameter that generates any difference between the real and the nominal term premium in the endogenous inflation case. I set the inflation target toπ¯$\overline{\pi }$= 0.005, which implies a 2% annual target. For the exogenous inflation expectation process, I set parametersσπ,θπ,πL$\sigma _{\pi }, \theta _{\pi }, \pi _{L}$, andπ¯$\overline{\pi }$such that the mean of inflation expectations is 0.9% per quarter to match the level of the nominal yield curve, and inflation spends 99% of the time in the range [−0.5%, 2%] in quarterly terms. I plot the invariant distribution for π in theInternet Appendix. Lastly, for simplicity, I set the correlationsϕ12=ϕ23=0$\phi _{12}=\phi _{23}=0$to focus solely on the correlation between persistent shocks to inflation expectations and shocks to the real economy. Then, as in the endogenous inflation case, only one parameter dictates any difference between the real yield curve and the nominal yield curve, which, in the exogenous inflation case, is ϕ13. I setϕ13=−0.5$\phi _{13}=-0.5$, which implies a correlation between aggregate endowment and inflation expectations,corr(dlog(y),π)$\text{{\it corr}}(dlog(y), \pi )$, of −0.21. I also provide results withϕ13=−0.2$\phi _{13}=-0.2$, which impliescorr(dlog(y),π)$\text{{\it corr}}(dlog(y), \pi )$= −0.08. These numbers forcorr(dlog(y),π)$corr(dlog(y), \pi )$are broadly consistent with the correlation between consumption growth and inflation expectations in the data, which I find to be −0.13.14
The Economic Mechanism in the Model. Figure1shows the solution of the relevant objects summarizing the economic mechanism. First, notice that both the real rate and the price of risk move in tandem across the state space: real interest rates are high (bond prices are low) when the aggregate price of risk is high. This is because, asxdecreases, asset prices reflect the preferences of the more risk-averse investors (low EIS and high RA) relatively more. In particular, as I elaborate in the next subsections, a low EIS translates into a higher real rate, whereas a high RA implies a higher price of risk.
As shown in Proposition3, negative aggregate shocks affect the less risk-averse investor's net worth relatively more, soxdecreases. When less risk-averse investors lose net worth, their ability to issue short-term debt at an aggregate level is reduced (as shown in the lower-middle panel). Thus, total credit as a fraction of total equity in the economy goes down (also depicted in the lower-middle panel). Additionally, there is an increase in the price of credit—the real rate —because the market has to compensate investors with a lower EIS to smooth consumption over time. Lastly, because relatively more risk-averse investors are clearing the market, the price of the risky asset goes down, and the aggregate price of risk increases (upper-middle and upper-right panels in Figure1).
The Real Yield Curve. Figure2shows the results for the term structure up to 80 quarters. On average, the real yield curve is upward sloping due to the presence of a positive term premium: real bonds are risky assets, since bond prices go down in states in which the price of risk increases (I discuss the term premium later). Interestingly, the yield curve features endogenous fluctuations across the business cycle. In particular, when there is a contraction in risk-tolerant investors' net worth (i.e., lowx), the level of the real yield curve increases, and its slope becomes negative. That is,xis negatively correlated with the level factor and positively correlated with the slope of the curve.
A useful way to further understand the model's term structure implications is to study the interest rate dynamics. To do so, I apply Itô's lemma tor(xt)$r(x_{t})$and study the associated drift and diffusion of the short rate,15shown in Panel B of Figure2. In particular, notice that the expected change inr,μr,t$\mu _{r,t}$, becomes more negative whenxdecreases; because real rates are mean reverting, when rates are high, they are expected to fall. The expectation that the short-term rate will decrease in the future is strong enough to imply the reversion of the slope in Panel A.
I next study the term premium, the covariance term (20). Figure3shows the model's prediction for this covariance. The left-hand panel shows the covariance across the state space for three different maturities (4, 20, and 80 quarters). The larger the horizon, the larger the premium the bond carries. Intuitively, the longer the horizon of the bond, the more likely it will lose value in a bad state at some point in its lifetime. The right-hand panel of the figure shows the mean term premium across horizons. This panel conveys the idea that long-term bonds are riskier than short-term bonds and therefore should pay a higher return on average.
Lastly, TableIIIdisplays the theoretical moments and illustrates the role of EIS heterogeneity in the model. As also shown in Figure2, the table demonstrates that the model matches the slope and volatility of the real term structure. The model captures the fact that the long-term real yields are highly volatile; they display a slightly smaller volatility than do short-term yields, a feature that is hard to achieve in standard representative-agent economies. The table also illustrates the risk-sharing mechanism that drives this result. Under the baseline calibration, A-type investors consume 0.0108 of their net worth (represented byξA$\xi _{A}$), whereas B-type investors consume a higher fraction, 0.0156. This is basically due to the fact that A-type investors are operating with leverage, so they consume a smaller fraction of their net worth on average. Also, because B-type investors feature a lower EIS, their consumption-to-wealth ratio is more volatile than that of A-type investors. This implies that investors are sharing aggregate risk, a measure denoted byRin the table.
When B-type investors feature CRRA preferences—that is,ψB=1/γB$\psi _{B}=1/\gamma _{B}$—the gains from sharing risk are lower: fluctuations inxhave a relatively similar effect on investors' utility (Ris close to zero). TableIIIshows that the consumption-to-wealth ratio of both agents is relatively similar to and less volatile than that in the baseline calibration. This implies that leveraged investors are borrowing against investors who have a similar willingness to smooth consumption intertemporally, and therefore, the equilibrium spot rate does not fluctuate much. As a consequence, the volatility of yields is roughly three times lower than in the data, and the slope of the yield curve is significantly smaller: long-term bonds are less risky, since interest rates (and real bond prices) do not fluctuate much.
In the case ofψB=ψA$\psi _{B}=\psi _{A}$, investors are heterogeneous along the RA dimension only. This implies that the volatility of real yields is virtually zero, and the slope of the real yield curve is almost flat (indeed, the curve is slightly downward sloping). Intuitively, this is because wealth fluctuates among agents with the exact same willingness to smooth consumption across time, and therefore, the adjustment in the real rates needs to be much smaller to clear the markets.
Cyclicality ofrt$r_{t}$:Intuition. A central piece in the mechanism is thatrincreases whenxdecreases,rx<0$r_{x}<0$, in states in which the price of risk is high. This is important because if persistently higher rates (or lower real bond prices) occur when the price of risk is high (i.e., lowx), then real bonds should carry a positive real term premium. That is, the marginal investor requires a premium to hold an asset that goes down in value when marginal utility is high.
It is worth noting that the positive correlation between real rates and the price of risk translates into a positive correlation between news in real rates and news in the real bond risk premium. Additionally, the model implies a positive correlation between bonds and stocks, which translates into a positive correlation between news in real rates and the risk premium on equities. Campbell, Pflueger, and Viceira (2020) develop a habits model that can produce mixed results regarding the correlation between real rates and risk premiums on bonds and equities. In particular, their model generates a positive correlation between real rate news and the equity risk premium in the 1980 to 2000 sample but a negative one in the post-2000 sample. However, their model implies a positive correlation between news in the risk premium on nominal bonds and real rate news, as in my model.
Inspecting the Role ofψB$\psi _{B}$. I next evaluate the importance ofψB$\psi _{B}$in driving the term structure results. In particular, I show how low levels ofψB$\psi _{B}$produce substantial nonlinearities in the real interest rate, generating a large average term premium (and a steep yield curve). Keeping all other parameters to the baseline calibration, I explore a wide range of values forψB$\psi _{B}$, including cases in whichψB>γB−1$\psi _{B}>\gamma _{B}^{-1}$(preference for early resolution of uncertainty),ψB=γB−1$\psi _{B}=\gamma _{B}^{-1}$(indifference to the resolution of uncertainty), andψB<γB−1$\psi _{B}<\gamma _{B}^{-1}$(preference for late resolution of uncertainty).
Because of the lack of a closed-form solution, I first use an approximation to understand howψB$\psi _{B}$affectsrx$r_{x}$, following the example shown in (28). Then, I use the numerical solution to show the link betweenrx$r_{x}$and the term structure. These two steps connectψB$\psi _{B}$to the term structure dynamics.
The lower-left panel in Figure4shows (29) for different values ofψB$\psi _{B}$, using the baseline calibration for other parameters. The upper-left panel in the figure shows the unconditionalrx$r_{x}$in the model. Both the approximation (29) and the derivative in the model display a similar pattern:rx$r_{x}$becomes very negative whenψB$\psi _{B}$is small. This is because, at low levels ofψB$\psi _{B}$, changes inxaffect the IS term much more than the PS term. Notice that the derivativeE[rx]$E[r_{x}]$can be negative even whenψB>γB−1$\psi _{B}>\gamma _{B}^{-1}$, which is the case when both investors prefer early resolution of uncertainty. This indicates that while investors' individual consumption displays endogenously persistent dynamics, investor B's preference resolution of uncertainty does not itself contribute to a positive or negative term premium. Rather, it is how the low levels of the EIS generate substantial nonlinearities inr(and thus in real bonds) that drives results in the term structure. Notice that the price of risk (lower-right panel) is barely affected byψB$\psi _{B}$.
The Nominal Yield Curve with Exogenous Inflation. Figure5shows the results for the nominal yield curve when inflation follows an exogenous stochastic process, (21). The left panel fixesxat the steady-state value and displays the nominal yield curve for different values of inflation expectations in the bivariate density (as shown in theInternet Appendix). On average, the yield curve is upward sloping because both the real component and the nominal components render nominal bonds risky assets, as shown in equation (23). I discuss the decomposition between these two sources below. The real source of risk is driven by the forces in the real yield curve, discussed previously. The nominal source of risk comes fromϕ13<0$\phi _{13}<0$: an exogenous sequence of positive inflation expectation shocks is associated with negative shocks to the real economy. This means that inflation is expected to erode the purchasing power of nominal payments precisely when the marginal investor values those resources the most.21Therefore, nominal bonds are risky.
To understand the role of inflation risk, driven by ϕ13, I next study the decomposition of the nominal and real components of (23). This is a useful analysis because in models in which the real term premium is zero (or negative), the nominal-term premium would be driven mostly by inflation risk. I also provide a decomposition of nominal yields' news below, following the methodology proposed in Duffee (2018).
Figure6illustrates this decomposition of an 80-quarter nominal bond.22On the left-hand side, the figure depicts the real and nominal components across thexdimension (i.e., fixing π at different values); on the right-hand side, I show the real and nominal components across the π dimension (i.e., fixingxat different values).
On average, the real component explains about 80% of the nominal term premium. As shown in the upper-left panel, an increase inxreduces the real component. This is because effective RA decreases asxincreases and fewer risk-averse investors rebuild their balance sheets. This is scaled by the level of π: The greater is π (gray line), the smaller is the real component (nominal risk becomes relatively more important).
The Nominal Term Structure Curve with Endogenous Inflation. Motivated by the previous decomposition, in which the real component drives the nominal term premium, I next study the nominal term structure with an endogenous inflation process. As shown in equation (25), the endogenous inflation process depends on policy parameters δ0andδπ$\delta _{\pi }$as well as on the real interest rater(x)$r(x)$. In particular, whenδπ>1$\delta _{\pi }>1$, the nominal interest rate moves in the same direction as the real interest rate by a factor ofδπδπ−1>1$\frac{\delta _{\pi }}{\delta _{\pi }-1}>1$.
The difference in the magnitudes implies that the monetary authority anchors inflation expectations by adjusting the policy instrument more than one-to-one to fluctuations in the real economy (represented byr(x)$r(x)$), and this is captured by fluctuations in nominal bond prices. In other words, the sensitivity of nominal bond prices to fluctuations inxwill be higher than that of real bond prices. Put differently, the derivative with respect toxis higher in a nominal bond than in a real bond:Px$(x,T)>Px(x,T)∀(x,T)$P_{x}^{\$}(x,T) >P_{x}(x,T) \text{ }\forall (x,T)$. This implies that the Taylor coefficient magnifies the positive real term premium. Figure7, Panel A, shows the results for the nominal term structure under endogenous inflation. The average nominal yield curve, withδπ=1.5$\delta _{\pi }=1.5$, is upward sloping and in line with the evidence. Indeed, the slope of the nominal term structure in the figure is higher than that in Figure2(almost twice as high, as in the data), precisely because of the effect ofδπδπ−1$\frac{\delta _{\pi }}{\delta _{\pi }-1}$and its effect on the derivative of bond prices.
In Panel B of Figure7, I show the properties of endogenous inflation expectations and the corresponding nominal yield curves. The lower the Taylor coefficientδπ$\delta _{\pi }$(which can be interpreted as a relatively “loose” monetary rule), the greater the unconditional mean and standard deviation of inflation expectations. A lower coefficient then translates into a steeper nominal yield curve because monetary policy reacts relatively less to changes in the real economy, which implies a more volatile inflation rate and thus a greater derivativePx$,(T)(x,t)$P_{x}^{\$,(T) }(x,t)$. This can be seen on the right-hand side of Panel B in Figure7, where I show the normalized yield curves.
Nominal Yield Curve: Theoretical Moments and Alternative Parametrizations. TableIV, Panel B, reports the models' implied moments for the nominal yield curve under alternative values of the key parameters. In the endogenous inflation setup, the key parameter isδπ$\delta _{\pi }$because it captures the sensitivity of nominal yields to the real component (summarized byx). The table shows the yields' mean and standard deviation of the endogenous inflation setup in rows (a) and (b). Using plausible and well-established values for the Taylor coefficientδπ$\delta _{\pi }$, 1.5 and 1.7, the model can well capture the yields' first and second moments of the nominal yield curve.
The top panels of Figure8show the decomposition of nominal yields for the endogenous and exogenous inflation cases, in the baseline calibration. Qualitatively, both inflation models display a pattern close to the evidence reported in Duffee (2018): Inflation news does not explain the vast majority of news in nominal yields. Indeed, in the exogenous inflation model (upper-right panel), long-term nominal yields (longer than 40 quarters) are driven mostly by news to real rates, a feature that can be observed by the red circles (inflation news) moving below the solid black line (real news) around the 40-quarter maturity. As discussed in Duffee (2018), many leading macrofinance models would imply that the red circles (inflation news) overlap with the blue circles (total news), which is at odds with the data and has important consequences for our understanding about what drives long-term nominal yields.
Notice that the decomposition of the endogenous inflation model shows a different pattern than that in the exogenous inflation model. This is because in the former, there are no shocks to inflation per se—all shocks are real. As discussed earlier, the fact that the Taylor coefficient is greater than 1 makes inflation more sensitive to real shocks, and thus, the inflation news is proportional to real news.
The bottom two panels show the corresponding variance ratios. These are the ratios of the unconditional variance of inflation shocks to the total variance of nominal yield shocks in the endogenous (lower-left panel) and exogenous (lower-right panel) models. Duffee (2018) reports evidence showing a ratio around 0.15 for a 10-year nominal bond. Both the endogenous and exogenous inflation models in the baseline calibration produce considerably low variance ratios, in the range of 0.3, much better performance than other leading macrofinance models reviewed in Duffee (2018), which produce variance ratios very close to one.
Duffee (2018) shows that the habits model presented in Wachter (2006) can also produce variance ratios and shock decompositions that are close to the evidence. Indeed, the decomposition in the exogenous inflation case (upper-right panel in Figure8) is qualitatively similar to that in Wachter (2006), which is also an exogenous inflation model (reported in figure 10 in Duffee (2018)). The heterogeneous agents framework I presented, however, produces a term structure of yield volatility that is downward sloping (i.e., long-term yields are slightly less volatile than short-term yields), as in the data, whereas Wachter (2006) implies the opposite (i.e., more volatile long-term than short-term yields), as noted in Duffee (2018) and Le, Singleton, and Dai (2010).
Figure9shows the results for the regression in the model and in the data. The left-side panels show the results for the long-rate regression (31), and the right-side panels show the results for the short-rate regression (32). From the left-side panels, it can be seen that the model has a negativeβL$\beta _{L}$in the regressions with real rates (upper-left panel), with nominal rates using endogenous inflation (middle-left panel), and with nominal rates using exogenous inflation (lower-left panel). It is clear from these results that the expectation hypothesis fails in the model.
This is due to the highly nonlinear dynamics in term premiums and changes in long-term interest rates. More precisely, the convexity of the interest rate with respect to the state variable produces nonlinear speeds of mean reversion in interest rates (i.e., rates are expected to mean-revert faster when they are high than when they are low). In particular, on average, the slope of the yield curve is positively correlated with term premiums and negatively correlated with expected changes in long-term rates.24
Results with the short-rate regressions (32), in the right-side panels of Figure9, indicate somewhat less conclusive evidence against the expectation hypothesis. The coefficients in the model, however, indicate a failure of the expectation hypothesis across maturities.
The only choice that remains is the value of the endogenous state variable,x, to initialize the model in 1971:Q2. To do so, I set the initial value at the unconditional mean ofx, which is indeed close to the level ofxthat minimizes the distance between the model-implied series and the actual data.25Using the unconditional mean ofxas an initial value removes any mean-reverting force throughout the sample.
Figure10shows the results for a 10-year maturity (results for other maturities are similar) over the Full Sample. The upper panels show the model-implied series for the nominal and real 10-year yields and their counterparts in the data. Despite its single-factor structure, the model is able to capture a good fraction of the fluctuations in yields in Short Sample II (1971:Q3 to 2007:Q4). However, while the sample moments in the data are similar in the Full Sample and in Short Sample II (as can be seen in TableI)—and hence the model can capture those—the implied paths are counterfactual after 2009. In particular, model-implied yields persistently spike after 2009 because the sequence of negative macro shocks around 2008 causes a persistent negative two-standard-deviation move inx.
article.VI. Conclusion:
Understanding the properties of nominal and real yield curves in a unified framework has been a long-standing challenge for macroeconomists and financial economists. In this paper, I propose a model in which investor heterogeneity is key for understanding the salient properties of the U.S. real and nominal term structure. To do so, I depart from the representative-agent framework and study a general equilibrium term structure model with heterogeneous investors who differ in their attitudes toward risk and, importantly, in their willingness to substitute consumption over time.
A distinctive feature of the model is its ability to produce substantial nonlinearities in expected real rates and term premiums, generating an average upward-sloping term structure and highly volatile long-term yields, consistent with the evidence. I find that accounting for differences in investors' willingness to substitute consumption over time is critical for these results. As elaborated in the text, high-marginal-utility states are associated with a low willingness to smooth consumption in the economy. As a consequence, the real interest rate increases (bond prices decrease) in states of high marginal utility. Therefore, real bonds are risky assets: they decrease in value in states in which the marginal investor values consumption the most. These high-marginal-utility states are driven by a reduction in leveraged investors' net worth, which, in turn, correlates with a reduction in the aggregate amount of credit in the economy.
Besides capturing the main moments of the real and nominal yield curve dynamics (without ignoring the equity premium), the model rationalizes several important elements of the yields data. For example, the model predicts that news in nominal yields is driven mostly by news in real rates and news in term premiums, a feature that has been challenging to rationalize in representative-agent models (Duffee (2018)). Additionally, the model's implied series for the endogenous state variable (produced after introducing macro shocks from the data) have substantial comovement with the level of real and nominal yields in the data.
Because the model has a single state variable, several relevant aspects of the data cannot be rationalized. For example, the model implies a positive correlation between equities and bond returns, a feature that has not been observed in long samples. Additionally, because of this positive correlation, the model also implies that observable information about equities, such as the dividend-to-price ratio, should predict bond returns, which is at odds with the evidence. Lastly, the model implies a path for real and nominal rates that include a sharp and persistent spike after 2009, which is at odds with the observed paths in the data. These issues could potentially be addressed by extending the model with additional state variables (for example, as in Campbell, Pflueger, and Viceira (2020), who extend the habits model with the output gap as an additional state variable), or with cohort-specific shocks (Gârleanu and Panageas (2021)). Tackling these important issues is left for future research.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.A.5. Numerical Procedure:
I use a spectral collocation method based on Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind to numerically solve the model. I provide the details below. This technique delivers a highly accurate global solution.
Conceptually, the numerical solution consists of representing the unknown functions as Chebyshev polynomials and then substituting them (with their corresponding derivatives) into the differential equations characterizing the equilibrium. In particular, I solve for: (i) two value functions (ξA$\xi _{A}$andξB$\xi _{B}$), (ii) the endowment claim, (iii) the initial earnings, (iv) real bond prices, and (v) nominal bond prices. The system of equations consists of:26(i) two Hamilon-Jacobi-Bellman equations (14), (ii) the no-arbitrage condition for the endowment claim (C), (iii) the pricing equation for the initial earnings (7), (iv) the pricing equation for real bond prices (19), and (v) the pricing equation for nominal bond prices with exogenous inflation (22).
